name: Business Ethics & Critical Thinking
nameZh: 商业伦理与批判性思维
description: Develops ethical reasoning and critical thinking skills in business contexts — moral frameworks, stakeholder theory, corporate responsibility, logical fallacies, decision-making under uncertainty, and real-world case studies.

modules.title: Critical Thinking Foundations
modules.objectives: Define critical thinking; identify and evaluate assumptions; assess quality of evidence; apply logical reasoning to everyday arguments.
modules.readingNote: MIT OpenCourseWare — Problems in Philosophy: Introduction to Argument and Evidence
modules.videoNote: Crash Course Philosophy #2 — Reasoning and Argumentation
modules.video2Note: TED-Ed — How to spot a misleading graph (critical evaluation of evidence)
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Logic and Critical Thinking exercises
modules.assignment: Find one news article and one advertisement. Identify the main claim, the evidence provided, and at least two hidden assumptions in each. Write a one-page evaluation of whether the evidence sufficiently supports the claim.

modules.title: Logical Fallacies
modules.objectives: Recognize and name common logical fallacies including ad hominem, straw man, false dichotomy, slippery slope, appeal to authority, and bandwagon; explain why each is flawed.
modules.readingNote: MIT OpenCourseWare — Problems in Philosophy: Fallacies and Bad Arguments
modules.video2Note: TED-Ed — Can you outsmart the fallacy? (Interactive fallacy identification)
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Spotting logical fallacies in arguments
modules.assignment: Collect three real-world examples of logical fallacies from news, social media, or advertisements (one per week). For each, name the fallacy, explain why it is fallacious, and rewrite the argument to make it logically valid.

modules.title: Ethical Frameworks
modules.objectives: Explain the core tenets of utilitarianism, deontology, virtue ethics, and care ethics; apply each framework to a business scenario and evaluate the resulting recommendation.
modules.readingNote: MIT OpenCourseWare — Problems in Philosophy: Normative Ethics readings
modules.videoNote: Crash Course Philosophy #36 — Kant and Categorical Imperatives (Deontology)
modules.video2Note: TED-Ed — What is the utilitarian calculus? Explaining utilitarianism
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Ethics and Society: moral frameworks overview
modules.assignment: Present one business dilemma (e.g., outsourcing jobs to cut costs). Analyze the dilemma through all four ethical frameworks. Write a 400-word essay arguing which framework produces the most defensible business decision and why.

modules.title: Stakeholder Theory vs. Shareholder Theory
modules.titleZh: 利益相关者理论与股东理论
modules.objectives: Articulate Friedman's shareholder primacy argument and Freeman's stakeholder theory; evaluate each position's ethical and practical strengths and weaknesses; apply both frameworks to a real company decision.
modules.readingNote: MIT OpenCourseWare — Technology Strategy: Stakeholder and Shareholder readings
modules.videoNote: TED-Ed — What is shareholder theory? (Milton Friedman explained)
modules.video2Note: TED-Ed — Why should businesses care about stakeholders? (Freeman's stakeholder theory)
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Social Entrepreneurship and stakeholder thinking
modules.assignment: Choose a recent corporate decision (e.g., a layoff announcement, a product recall, or a donation campaign). Identify all stakeholders affected and analyze the decision from both Friedman's and Freeman's perspectives. Conclude with your own reasoned position in 300 words.

modules.title: Corporate Social Responsibility (CSR)
modules.objectives: Explain Carroll's CSR Pyramid and ESG investing criteria; distinguish CSR from philanthropy and social enterprise; evaluate a company's CSR program for authenticity and impact.
modules.readingNote: MIT OpenCourseWare — Leadership Stories: Corporate Responsibility and Ethics readings
modules.videoNote: TED-Ed — How do companies decide to be socially responsible?
modules.video2Note: Crash Course Business: Soft Skills #5 — Corporate Social Responsibility
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Social Enterprise and measuring social impact
modules.assignment: Research the CSR report of one publicly traded company. Score the company on Carroll's four CSR dimensions (economic, legal, ethical, philanthropic). Write a one-page critical assessment of whether their stated commitments are backed by measurable actions.

modules.title: Business Ethics in Practice
modules.objectives: Identify common workplace ethical issues including conflicts of interest, bribery, and discrimination; explain whistleblower protections; describe traits of ethical leadership.
modules.readingNote: MIT OpenCourseWare — Leadership Stories: Ethics and Authority in the Workplace
modules.videoNote: TED-Ed — The ethical dilemma of whistleblowing
modules.video2Note: TED Talk — Moral Ambition: Why more people should speak up at work (Rutger Bregman)
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Ethics in the workplace: scenario exercises
modules.assignment: Role-play scenario: Your manager asks you to falsify expense reports. Write a 350-word response describing what you would do, referencing at least two ethical frameworks and any relevant legal protections for whistleblowers in your country.

modules.title: Environmental Ethics in Business
modules.objectives: Define ecological responsibility and sustainability ethics; identify greenwashing tactics; evaluate a company's environmental claims against measurable standards such as the Paris Agreement or GRI.
modules.readingNote: MIT OpenCourseWare — Environmental Ethics: Ecological Responsibility and Sustainability
modules.videoNote: TED-Ed — What is greenwashing, and how can you spot it?
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Human Impacts on the Environment: sustainability concepts
modules.assignment: Select one company that has made major sustainability claims (e.g., carbon neutrality, zero-waste). Gather three pieces of evidence supporting the claim and three pieces that challenge it. Write a verdict on whether the claim constitutes genuine action or greenwashing.

modules.title: Technology & Ethics
modules.objectives: Explain data privacy rights and GDPR principles; define algorithmic bias and give real-world examples; articulate ethical concerns around AI decision-making and surveillance capitalism.
modules.readingNote: MIT OpenCourseWare — Ethics of AI and Automated Systems supplementary readings
modules.videoNote: TED-Ed — How algorithms shape our world (Kevin Slavin)
modules.video2Note: Crash Course AI #18 — Algorithmic Bias and Fairness
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Data, Bias, and Ethics in Technology
modules.assignment: Investigate one real case of algorithmic bias (e.g., hiring algorithms, facial recognition, credit scoring). Describe how the bias arose, who was harmed, and propose three technical or policy changes that could reduce the bias.

modules.title: Global Ethics
modules.objectives: Distinguish cultural relativism from universal ethical standards; analyze labor rights issues in global supply chains; evaluate multinational companies' responsibilities in low-wage markets.
modules.readingNote: MIT OpenCourseWare — Leadership Stories: Global Ethics and Cross-Cultural Management
modules.videoNote: TED-Ed — Supply chains and globalization: the true cost of cheap goods
modules.video2Note: Crash Course Philosophy #3 — Cultural Relativism vs. Moral Universalism
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Global ethics and multinational responsibility scenarios
modules.assignment: Research the labor conditions in one specific industry's global supply chain (e.g., fast fashion, electronics, agriculture). Identify the ethical tensions and write a 400-word argument either for or against a legally enforceable international minimum wage standard.

modules.title: Marketing Ethics
modules.objectives: Identify deceptive advertising techniques; explain FTC disclosure requirements for influencer marketing; evaluate the ethics of targeting vulnerable populations such as children or those with addictions.
modules.readingNote: MIT OpenCourseWare — Marketing Management: Ethics in Advertising and Promotion
modules.videoNote: TED-Ed — This is your brain on advertising
modules.video2Note: Crash Course Business: Soft Skills #11 — Marketing Ethics and Manipulation
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Marketing and advertising ethics case studies
modules.assignment: Analyze two advertisements targeting children or teenagers. Identify any deceptive or manipulative techniques used. Write a recommendation to a hypothetical regulatory agency on whether each ad should be modified or banned, citing ethical reasoning.

modules.title: Financial Ethics
modules.objectives: Define insider trading, accounting fraud, predatory lending, and Ponzi schemes; explain regulatory bodies such as the SEC; compare ethical investing models including ESG and impact investing.
modules.readingNote: MIT OpenCourseWare — Financial Management: Ethics in Finance readings
modules.videoNote: TED-Ed — What is insider trading, and why is it illegal?
modules.video2Note: Crash Course Economics #36 — Financial Markets and Ethical Failures
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Investment vehicles and ethical investing overview
modules.assignment: Research one financial scandal (e.g., Bernie Madoff, Wells Fargo fake accounts, Lehman Brothers). Write a case analysis: what ethical violations occurred, who was harmed, what regulations were broken, and what systemic reforms were (or should have been) implemented.

modules.title: Decision-Making Under Uncertainty
modules.objectives: Apply structured ethical decision-making frameworks to uncertain scenarios; explain risk assessment and moral residue; evaluate trade-offs when no clearly right answer exists.
modules.readingNote: MIT OpenCourseWare — Problems in Philosophy: Moral Uncertainty and Decision Theory
modules.videoNote: Crash Course Philosophy #40 — Decision Theory and Moral Uncertainty
modules.video2Note: TED-Ed — The paradox of choice: how to make decisions under uncertainty
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Probability, risk, and reasoning in decision-making
modules.assignment: You are a hospital administrator deciding whether to deploy an experimental AI diagnostic tool that is 85% accurate but may exhibit racial bias. Using at least two ethical frameworks and a structured decision matrix, justify your decision in a 400-word memo.

modules.title: Case Studies in Business Ethics
modules.objectives: Analyze the ethical failures of Enron, Volkswagen emissions scandal, Nike sweatshops, and Facebook/Cambridge Analytica; identify systemic causes of corporate misconduct and propose preventive governance structures.
modules.readingNote: MIT OpenCourseWare — Leadership Stories: Corporate Scandal Case Studies
modules.videoNote: TED-Ed — The rise and fall of Enron: a lesson in corporate ethics
modules.video2Note: Crash Course Business: Soft Skills — Corporate Ethics Failures (Volkswagen, Facebook)
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Corporate governance and ethical compliance case exercises
modules.assignment: Choose any two of the four case studies (Enron, Volkswagen, Nike, Facebook/Cambridge Analytica). For each, write a structured analysis: (1) what ethical rules were broken, (2) who was harmed and how, (3) what organizational culture allowed this to happen, and (4) what structural change would prevent recurrence.

modules.title: Ethics Capstone
modules.objectives: Select a real, unresolved ethical dilemma in business; apply multiple frameworks to analyze it; construct and defend a reasoned, evidence-based ethical position in writing.
modules.readingNote: MIT OpenCourseWare — Problems in Philosophy: Capstone: Building Ethical Arguments
modules.videoNote: TED Talk — How to argue well and defend an ethical position (Julia Dhar)
modules.video2Note: Crash Course Philosophy #48 — The Good Place and Moral Philosophy Review
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Ethics synthesis: applying frameworks to real dilemmas
modules.assignment: Identify a current ethical controversy in business that has not been fully resolved (e.g., AI replacing workers, gig economy classification, pharmaceutical pricing). Write a 600-word position paper that: defines the dilemma, presents the strongest counterargument, analyzes using at least two frameworks, and defends a clear, reasoned position with evidence.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes critical thinking?
options: Accepting expert opinions without question
Evaluating claims using logic, evidence, and careful reasoning
Memorizing facts from textbooks
Forming opinions based on personal feelings alone
questions.answer: Evaluating claims using logic, evidence, and careful reasoning
questions.explanation: Critical thinking involves actively and systematically evaluating evidence and arguments rather than accepting claims uncritically.

questions.question: A company executive argues, 'Our environmental policy must be wrong because the activist criticizing it was arrested last year.' This is an example of which fallacy?
options: Straw man
False dichotomy
Ad hominem
Slippery slope
questions.answer: Ad hominem
questions.explanation: Ad hominem attacks the person making the argument rather than addressing the argument's substance.

questions.question: The slippery slope fallacy assumes that:
options: Two options are the only possible choices
One event will inevitably lead to a chain of negative consequences
An authority figure's opinion automatically proves a claim
The speaker's character disproves their argument
questions.answer: One event will inevitably lead to a chain of negative consequences
questions.explanation: The slippery slope fallacy claims that one action will automatically set off a series of negative events without sufficient evidence for that chain of causation.

questions.question: According to utilitarianism, the morally correct action is the one that:
options: Follows a universal rule regardless of consequences
Produces the greatest good for the greatest number
Reflects the character of a virtuous person
Prioritizes care for those in close relationships
questions.answer: Produces the greatest good for the greatest number
questions.explanation: Utilitarianism, associated with Bentham and Mill, judges actions by their outcomes and aims to maximize overall well-being.

questions.question: Kant's deontological ethics holds that the moral worth of an action depends on:
options: The consequences it produces
The intention and the universal rule it follows
The happiness it creates for most people
The cultural norms of the actor's society
questions.answer: The intention and the universal rule it follows
questions.explanation: Kant's Categorical Imperative judges actions by whether they follow a duty-based rule that could be universalized for everyone.

questions.question: Milton Friedman argued that the primary social responsibility of a business is to:
options: Balance the needs of all stakeholders equally
Use its resources to engage in activities designed to increase its profits
Minimize environmental impact
Provide jobs and support local communities
questions.answer: Use its resources to engage in activities designed to increase its profits
questions.explanation: Friedman's shareholder primacy view holds that managers are agents of shareholders and should maximize profits within legal and ethical rules.

questions.question: R. Edward Freeman's stakeholder theory defines stakeholders as:
options: Only shareholders who own company stock
Any group or individual who can affect or is affected by the achievement of the firm's objectives
Government regulators and legal authorities only
Employees and customers but not suppliers
questions.answer: Any group or individual who can affect or is affected by the achievement of the firm's objectives
questions.explanation: Freeman's stakeholder theory broadens responsibility beyond shareholders to include employees, customers, suppliers, communities, and others.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT one of the four dimensions in Carroll's CSR Pyramid?
options: Economic responsibilities
Legal responsibilities
Philanthropic responsibilities
Political responsibilities
questions.answer: Political responsibilities
questions.explanation: Carroll's CSR Pyramid consists of economic, legal, ethical, and philanthropic responsibilities. Political responsibility is not one of the four layers.

questions.question: A conflict of interest in the workplace occurs when:
options: Two employees disagree about a business strategy
An employee's personal interests could improperly influence their professional decisions
A company faces competing demands from customers and suppliers
A manager must choose between two equally qualified candidates
questions.answer: An employee's personal interests could improperly influence their professional decisions
questions.explanation: A conflict of interest arises when a personal benefit could bias an employee's judgment in their official duties.

questions.question: Greenwashing refers to:
options: Genuine efforts by companies to reduce their carbon footprint
Misleading consumers about the environmental benefits of a product or practice
Government audits of corporate environmental claims
Investing in renewable energy projects
questions.answer: Misleading consumers about the environmental benefits of a product or practice
questions.explanation: Greenwashing involves making false or exaggerated environmental claims to appear more sustainable than a company actually is.

questions.question: The 'straw man' fallacy involves:
options: Attacking the person rather than their argument
Misrepresenting someone's position to make it easier to attack
Asserting that only two options exist when others are possible
Assuming that accepting one step leads inevitably to extreme consequences
questions.answer: Misrepresenting someone's position to make it easier to attack
questions.explanation: The straw man fallacy distorts or exaggerates the opponent's argument to defeat an easier version of it rather than the actual position.

questions.question: Virtue ethics focuses primarily on:
options: The consequences of individual actions
Adherence to universal moral rules
The character and moral virtues of the person acting
The greatest happiness for the greatest number
questions.answer: The character and moral virtues of the person acting
questions.explanation: Virtue ethics, rooted in Aristotle, evaluates the moral character of the agent rather than rules or outcomes.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes ESG investing?
options: Investing only in government bonds for stability
Evaluating companies based on Environmental, Social, and Governance criteria alongside financial performance
A strategy that maximizes short-term returns regardless of corporate behavior
An approach that focuses exclusively on avoiding tobacco stocks
questions.answer: Evaluating companies based on Environmental, Social, and Governance criteria alongside financial performance
questions.explanation: ESG investing integrates environmental, social, and governance factors into investment analysis to assess sustainability and ethical impact.

questions.question: Describe one similarity and one key difference between utilitarianism and Kantian deontology. Then give a brief example of a business decision where the two frameworks would lead to different conclusions.
questions.answer: Similarity: both are systematic moral frameworks aiming to guide ethical conduct. Difference: utilitarianism judges actions by their consequences (greatest good), while Kantian ethics judges by whether the action follows a universal duty regardless of outcome. Example: lying to a client to close a sale — utilitarianism might permit it if it maximizes happiness, while Kantian ethics forbids it because lying cannot be universalized.
questions.explanation: Strong answers contrast outcome-based vs. duty-based reasoning and apply the distinction concretely to a business context.

questions.question: A whistleblower at a pharmaceutical company discovers that clinical trial data was manipulated to gain FDA approval for a drug. Using any one ethical framework studied in this course, explain whether the employee has a moral obligation to report the misconduct, and identify one real-world risk they would face by doing so.
questions.answer: Using deontology: the employee has a duty to protect public safety and tell the truth regardless of personal cost — reporting is morally obligatory. Using utilitarianism: reporting prevents widespread patient harm and maximizes overall welfare, so reporting is the right action. Real-world risk: retaliation, termination, legal costs, or professional blacklisting despite laws like the Dodd-Frank Act offering some protections.
questions.explanation: Full credit for applying a framework consistently, articulating the obligation clearly, and naming a realistic risk.

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of a false dichotomy?
options: You either support our company's data practices or you have nothing to hide.
Our product is cheaper than the competition's.
Studies show 80% of users prefer our interface.
The CEO has a Harvard degree, so his plan must be sound.
questions.answer: You either support our company's data practices or you have nothing to hide.
questions.explanation: A false dichotomy presents only two options when more exist — here, many positions between full support and 'nothing to hide' are possible.

questions.question: Care ethics, associated with Carol Gilligan, emphasizes:
options: Universal duties that apply regardless of relationships
Maximizing aggregate happiness across society
Moral obligations arising from relationships and the needs of particular others
Adherence to legal standards as the baseline of ethics
questions.answer: Moral obligations arising from relationships and the needs of particular others
questions.explanation: Care ethics holds that morality is rooted in relationships and responsibilities to specific others, not abstract universal rules.

questions.question: Which argument most accurately represents the Freeman–Friedman debate?
options: Friedman believes businesses owe nothing to society; Freeman believes businesses should be nationalized.
Friedman says managers serve shareholders; Freeman says managers must balance multiple stakeholder interests.
Both Friedman and Freeman agree that profit is the sole purpose of business.
Freeman argues only customers and employees matter; Friedman includes all social groups.
questions.answer: Friedman says managers serve shareholders; Freeman says managers must balance multiple stakeholder interests.
questions.explanation: The core debate contrasts shareholder primacy (Friedman) with stakeholder inclusivity (Freeman) as the proper goal of business management.

questions.question: Which statement about whistleblowing is most accurate?
options: It is always illegal under corporate confidentiality agreements.
It refers to reporting internal misconduct to an appropriate authority, and legal protections exist in many jurisdictions.
It only applies to government employees, not private sector workers.
Whistleblowers are rarely harmed professionally after reporting.
questions.answer: It refers to reporting internal misconduct to an appropriate authority, and legal protections exist in many jurisdictions.
questions.explanation: Whistleblowing involves disclosing wrongdoing; laws such as the Dodd-Frank Act provide some protection, though retaliation still occurs in practice.

questions.question: Surveillance capitalism, as described by Shoshana Zuboff, refers to:
options: Government surveillance of suspected criminals using digital tools
The economic logic of capturing human behavioral data as a free raw material to predict and sell future behavior
Installing cameras in corporate offices to prevent theft
A regulatory framework for tech company accountability
questions.answer: The economic logic of capturing human behavioral data as a free raw material to predict and sell future behavior
questions.explanation: Zuboff defines surveillance capitalism as a new economic order where human experience is commodified and sold to modify future behavior for profit.

questions.question: Cultural relativism in ethics holds that:
options: All moral values are universal and apply across cultures
Moral standards are determined by cultural context and cannot be judged from the outside
Western ethical frameworks are superior to others
Ethics only apply when codified into law
questions.answer: Moral standards are determined by cultural context and cannot be judged from the outside
questions.explanation: Cultural relativism claims that ethical judgments are valid only within a given cultural framework, making cross-cultural moral criticism problematic.

questions.question: The FTC requires influencers to disclose paid partnerships primarily to:
options: Increase tax revenue from social media income
Ensure consumers can distinguish advertising from genuine personal endorsements
Limit the number of influencer partnerships per company
Protect influencers from being underpaid by brands
questions.answer: Ensure consumers can distinguish advertising from genuine personal endorsements
questions.explanation: FTC disclosure rules are designed to prevent deceptive advertising by ensuring audiences know when content is paid promotion.

questions.question: Insider trading is illegal primarily because it:
options: Reduces the overall volume of stock market transactions
Gives certain individuals an unfair advantage based on non-public information, undermining market integrity
Violates accounting standards set by the FASB
Leads directly to corporate bankruptcy
questions.answer: Gives certain individuals an unfair advantage based on non-public information, undermining market integrity
questions.explanation: Insider trading violates fairness principles by allowing some investors to profit from private information unavailable to the public.

questions.question: The concept of 'moral residue' in decision-making under uncertainty refers to:
options: The legal liability that remains after a lawsuit is settled
The lingering sense of moral regret even after making the best available choice
Ethical rules that are outdated but still on the books
The portion of a decision that cannot be rationally justified
questions.answer: The lingering sense of moral regret even after making the best available choice
questions.explanation: Moral residue acknowledges that even a justified decision may involve unavoidable harm, leaving a remainder of moral responsibility.

questions.question: In the Enron scandal, which ethical failure was most central to the company's collapse?
options: Failure to invest in new technologies
Systematic accounting fraud that hid debt and inflated profits, enabled by complicit auditors
Overpaying executive salaries beyond industry norms
Launching products into markets without regulatory approval
questions.answer: Systematic accounting fraud that hid debt and inflated profits, enabled by complicit auditors
questions.explanation: Enron used special purpose entities and mark-to-market accounting deceptively; its auditor Arthur Andersen also failed its gatekeeping role.

questions.question: The Volkswagen emissions scandal (Dieselgate) is primarily an example of:
options: Greenwashing through exaggerated CSR reporting
Deliberate engineering fraud to cheat environmental compliance tests
Financial misreporting to mislead investors
Supply chain labor violations in emerging markets
questions.answer: Deliberate engineering fraud to cheat environmental compliance tests
questions.explanation: VW installed 'defeat devices' in diesel vehicles that detected test conditions and altered engine performance to pass emissions standards it could not genuinely meet.

questions.question: The Cambridge Analytica scandal raised which primary ethical concern?
options: Insider trading in Facebook stock
Unauthorized harvesting and political misuse of millions of Facebook users' personal data
False advertising of Facebook's advertising reach
Discriminatory hiring practices at a data analytics firm
questions.answer: Unauthorized harvesting and political misuse of millions of Facebook users' personal data
questions.explanation: Cambridge Analytica obtained data on 87 million Facebook users without their consent and used it for targeted political advertising, raising major data privacy concerns.

questions.question: Algorithmic bias in AI systems most commonly results from:
options: Programming errors introduced intentionally by hackers
Training data that reflects historical human biases and inequalities
Failure to use sufficient computational power
Laws that prohibit fair data collection
questions.answer: Training data that reflects historical human biases and inequalities
questions.explanation: When AI systems are trained on biased historical data (e.g., biased hiring records), they learn and replicate those biases in their outputs.

questions.question: Which action would most directly address ethical concerns about labor conditions in global supply chains?
options: Raising executive compensation at the parent company
Conducting independent third-party audits of supplier factories and publishing the results
Expanding marketing campaigns in affected regions
Moving production to automated facilities without workers
questions.answer: Conducting independent third-party audits of supplier factories and publishing the results
questions.explanation: Independent audits and public transparency hold suppliers accountable and give consumers and investors reliable information about labor conditions.

questions.question: A structured ethical decision framework typically begins with which step?
options: Announcing the decision to stakeholders
Identifying who is affected and what ethical principles are at stake
Selecting the most profitable option among available choices
Consulting legal counsel to ensure compliance
questions.answer: Identifying who is affected and what ethical principles are at stake
questions.explanation: Sound ethical decision-making starts by clarifying the situation, identifying all affected parties, and determining which ethical principles are relevant before evaluating options.

questions.question: Which of the following is the strongest argument that corporations have ethical obligations beyond maximizing profit?
options: Profits are already maximized once all legal expenses are paid.
Corporations use public infrastructure and resources, so they owe obligations to the society that enables their existence.
Shareholders voluntarily give up their returns to fund social programs.
Regulations already ensure corporations act ethically, so additional obligations are unnecessary.
questions.answer: Corporations use public infrastructure and resources, so they owe obligations to the society that enables their existence.
questions.explanation: Corporations rely on public goods — educated workers, legal systems, roads — giving rise to reciprocal social obligations that extend beyond profit alone.

questions.question: Predatory lending practices are unethical primarily because they:
options: Charge interest rates below the market average
Target financially vulnerable individuals with loan terms that are deceptive or impossible to repay
Allow borrowers to pay off loans early without penalty
Require too much documentation from applicants
questions.answer: Target financially vulnerable individuals with loan terms that are deceptive or impossible to repay
questions.explanation: Predatory lending exploits information asymmetry and vulnerability, trapping borrowers in cycles of debt through deceptive or exploitative terms.

questions.question: The Nike sweatshop controversy of the 1990s revealed poor labor conditions in its overseas factories. Using stakeholder theory and one additional ethical framework, argue whether Nike had a moral obligation to improve those conditions even when local laws did not require it.
questions.answer: Using stakeholder theory: factory workers are stakeholders whose wellbeing Nike affects directly; the theory obligates Nike to consider their interests alongside profit. Using deontology: paying workers wages that do not cover basic survival violates the duty to treat people as ends in themselves. Conclusion: yes, Nike had a moral obligation beyond legal compliance because both frameworks ground duties that transcend local regulatory minimums.
questions.explanation: Full credit for correctly applying stakeholder theory, using a second framework consistently, and reaching a well-reasoned conclusion.

questions.question: You are the chief ethics officer of a tech company. Your engineering team has discovered that the company's hiring algorithm disproportionately screens out applications from women. The algorithm is currently in use. Outline the immediate steps you would take, applying one ethical framework to justify your prioritization of those steps.
questions.answer: Immediate steps: (1) suspend the algorithm pending review, (2) notify leadership and legal, (3) commission an independent audit of training data and outcomes, (4) implement human review for all pending applications, (5) develop bias-correction measures before redeployment. Framework (deontology): there is a duty not to discriminate — continuing to use a known biased tool treats women as less worthy of consideration, violating their rights regardless of whether it improves efficiency.
questions.explanation: Full credit for a concrete action plan, logical sequencing, and a consistent, accurate application of a named ethical framework.

questions.question: Compare and contrast the ethical issues raised by the Enron scandal and the Facebook/Cambridge Analytica scandal. Identify one systemic governance reform that could have prevented each, and explain why structural change — not just individual punishment — is essential in addressing corporate misconduct.
questions.answer: Enron: financial fraud enabled by weak auditing independence and board oversight failures. Reform: mandatory auditor rotation and stronger board independence requirements (Sarbanes-Oxley partially addressed this). Cambridge Analytica: data harvested without informed consent enabled by platform policies that monetized access to user data. Reform: requiring opt-in (not opt-out) data sharing and independent privacy audits. Structural change matters because individual punishment does not alter the incentive structures, culture, or oversight mechanisms that made the misconduct possible — without systemic reform, the next actor faces the same pressures and opportunities.
questions.explanation: Full credit for accurate descriptions of both scandals, a plausible reform for each, and a clear argument for why systemic change is necessary beyond punishment.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following is the best definition of critical thinking?
options: Memorizing facts from authoritative sources
Systematically evaluating claims using logic, evidence, and reason
Disagreeing with everything you read
Forming opinions based on emotions
quizQuestions.answer: Systematically evaluating claims using logic, evidence, and reason
quizQuestions.explanation: Critical thinking is the disciplined practice of actively analyzing, evaluating, and synthesizing information rather than passively accepting it.

quizQuestions.question: An assumption is best described as:
options: A proven fact supported by data
A claim that has been verified by an expert
A belief taken for granted without explicit proof
An argument that contains no flaws
quizQuestions.answer: A belief taken for granted without explicit proof
quizQuestions.explanation: Assumptions are unstated premises that an argument relies on but does not prove — identifying them is key to critical evaluation.

quizQuestions.question: Which question best reflects critical evaluation of evidence?
options: Who wrote this and what are their incentives?
Does this agree with what I already believe?
Is this the most recent article available?
How long is this source?
quizQuestions.answer: Who wrote this and what are their incentives?
quizQuestions.explanation: Questioning the source's credibility and potential bias is a fundamental step in evaluating whether evidence is reliable.

quizQuestions.question: A valid argument is one in which:
options: The conclusion is true regardless of the premises
If the premises are true, the conclusion must be true
The argument is convincing to most people
The premises and conclusion all express opinions
quizQuestions.answer: If the premises are true, the conclusion must be true
quizQuestions.explanation: Validity is a logical property: in a valid argument, the conclusion follows necessarily from the premises.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following would strengthen a business argument?
options: Adding more adjectives to make the claim sound confident
Citing peer-reviewed research and specific data
Using circular reasoning to restate the conclusion
Appealing to the audience's emotions only
quizQuestions.answer: Citing peer-reviewed research and specific data
quizQuestions.explanation: Strong arguments are supported by credible, specific evidence rather than rhetoric or emotional appeals alone.

quizQuestions.question: A CEO says, 'Our critics don't understand business — they've never run a company.' This is an example of:
options: Slippery slope
Ad hominem
False dichotomy
Appeal to tradition
quizQuestions.answer: Ad hominem
quizQuestions.explanation: Ad hominem attacks the person rather than addressing the substance of the criticism.

quizQuestions.question: Which statement is an example of the false dichotomy fallacy?
options: Either we cut costs by 30% or we will go bankrupt.
Our profits rose 10% last quarter due to new marketing.
Most employees prefer flexible hours over pay raises.
The competitor's product has three known defects.
quizQuestions.answer: Either we cut costs by 30% or we will go bankrupt.
quizQuestions.explanation: This presents only two extreme options when other possibilities (moderate cuts, new revenue streams) exist.

quizQuestions.question: The straw man fallacy involves:
options: Using a famous quote to support your argument
Distorting the opponent's position to make it easier to refute
Providing too much evidence for a simple claim
Appealing to popular opinion to prove a point
quizQuestions.answer: Distorting the opponent's position to make it easier to refute
quizQuestions.explanation: Straw man misrepresents the opposing view so that defeating the distorted version appears to defeat the real argument.

quizQuestions.question: Which is an example of the slippery slope fallacy?
options: If we allow employees to work from home one day, soon no one will ever come to the office and the company will collapse.
Our market share grew after we increased the advertising budget.
The new product launch succeeded because of thorough testing.
Customers prefer products with simpler packaging.
quizQuestions.answer: If we allow employees to work from home one day, soon no one will ever come to the office and the company will collapse.
quizQuestions.explanation: This assumes a chain of drastic consequences from a small initial step without evidence that the chain is inevitable.

quizQuestions.question: An appeal to authority is fallacious when:
options: The authority has relevant expertise in the area being discussed
The authority's credentials are verified and publicly known
The authority cited has no relevant expertise in the specific subject
The authority's opinion matches the scientific consensus
quizQuestions.answer: The authority cited has no relevant expertise in the specific subject
quizQuestions.explanation: An appeal to authority is only fallacious when the authority cited is not qualified in the relevant domain.

quizQuestions.question: Which ethical framework asks: 'What rule, if followed by everyone, would lead to the best outcomes?'
options: Kantian deontology
Utilitarianism
Virtue ethics
Care ethics
quizQuestions.answer: Utilitarianism
quizQuestions.explanation: Utilitarianism evaluates actions by their consequences — the action that maximizes overall good is morally correct.

quizQuestions.question: Kant's Categorical Imperative states that you should act only according to a maxim that:
options: Maximizes the happiness of those involved
You could at the same time will to be a universal law
Reflects the virtues of an ideal moral agent
Prioritizes care for those closest to you
quizQuestions.answer: You could at the same time will to be a universal law
quizQuestions.explanation: Kant's first formulation: act only on principles you could consistently want everyone to follow.

quizQuestions.question: Virtue ethics primarily asks which question?
options: What produces the greatest good?
What does the rule require?
What would a person of good character do?
What do those I care about need?
quizQuestions.answer: What would a person of good character do?
quizQuestions.explanation: Virtue ethics focuses on the character of the moral agent rather than rules or consequences.

quizQuestions.question: A business decides to lay off workers to maximize quarterly profits. A utilitarian would evaluate this action by:
options: Asking whether the CEO has the legal right to make this decision
Calculating the total harm to workers versus the total benefit to shareholders and consumers
Determining whether layoffs violate a universal moral duty
Considering what kind of character the decision reflects in leadership
quizQuestions.answer: Calculating the total harm to workers versus the total benefit to shareholders and consumers
quizQuestions.explanation: Utilitarianism aggregates all costs and benefits across all affected parties to determine the morally correct action.

quizQuestions.question: Which framework would most clearly oppose using a person merely as a means to increase profit without their consent?
options: Utilitarianism
Cultural relativism
Kantian deontology
Social contract theory
quizQuestions.answer: Kantian deontology
quizQuestions.explanation: Kant's second formulation of the Categorical Imperative requires treating people as ends in themselves, never merely as means.

quizQuestions.question: Friedman's view on corporate social responsibility is best summarized as:
options: Corporations should pursue profits while also funding community programs
The only social responsibility of business is to increase profits within the rules of the game
Businesses must balance the needs of all stakeholders equally
Corporations have a duty to lead social change
quizQuestions.answer: The only social responsibility of business is to increase profits within the rules of the game
quizQuestions.explanation: Friedman argued that diverting shareholder funds to social causes without their consent is a misuse of their property.

quizQuestions.question: Which group would stakeholder theory include that shareholder theory excludes from primary consideration?
options: Board of directors
Institutional investors
Local community residents affected by company pollution
Company founders
quizQuestions.answer: Local community residents affected by company pollution
quizQuestions.explanation: Freeman's stakeholder theory includes anyone affected by company decisions — communities, employees, suppliers — not just those with financial ownership.

quizQuestions.question: Which argument best supports Friedman's shareholder primacy view?
options: Managers are elected by employees and should serve their interests first
Managers are agents of shareholders who entrusted them with capital, so profit maximization is their fiduciary duty
Financial markets are too complex for ethical considerations to apply
Shareholders always know less than managers about what is best for the company
quizQuestions.answer: Managers are elected by shareholders who entrusted them with capital, so profit maximization is their fiduciary duty
quizQuestions.explanation: Friedman's core argument is agency-based: managers have a contractual obligation to owners (shareholders) to maximize returns.

quizQuestions.question: A company chooses to pay its overseas suppliers above the local minimum wage even though it is not required to. This decision is most consistent with:
options: Shareholder theory
Stakeholder theory
Cultural relativism
Laissez-faire economics
quizQuestions.answer: Stakeholder theory
quizQuestions.explanation: Considering the wellbeing of suppliers — a non-shareholder group — reflects stakeholder theory's broader view of corporate obligations.

quizQuestions.question: The Business Roundtable's 2019 Statement on the Purpose of a Corporation, signed by 181 CEOs, signaled a shift toward:
options: Pure shareholder primacy with no exceptions
Stakeholder-inclusive commitments to customers, employees, suppliers, communities, and shareholders
Government regulation of executive compensation
Reducing corporate tax obligations as a social benefit
quizQuestions.answer: Stakeholder-inclusive commitments to customers, employees, suppliers, communities, and shareholders
quizQuestions.explanation: The 2019 statement represented a formal departure from exclusive shareholder primacy toward acknowledging broader stakeholder responsibilities.

quizQuestions.question: According to Carroll's CSR Pyramid, which level of responsibility is the foundation that all other levels depend on?
options: Philanthropic
Ethical
Legal
Economic
quizQuestions.answer: Economic
quizQuestions.explanation: Carroll's pyramid places economic responsibility at the base — a business must be profitable to sustain all other responsibilities.

quizQuestions.question: ESG investing stands for:
options: Equity, Sustainability, and Growth
Environmental, Social, and Governance
Earnings, Stability, and Governance
Ethical, Social, and Global
quizQuestions.answer: Environmental, Social, and Governance
quizQuestions.explanation: ESG refers to three non-financial criteria used to evaluate a company's sustainability and ethical impact alongside financial performance.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following is the strongest indicator that a company's CSR program is genuine rather than performative?
options: Publishing a glossy annual CSR report
Setting measurable targets and publicly reporting progress against them
Donating to a high-profile charity once a year
Featuring environmental themes in advertising
quizQuestions.answer: Setting measurable targets and publicly reporting progress against them
quizQuestions.explanation: Genuine CSR is characterized by accountability mechanisms — specific, measurable goals with transparent progress reporting.

quizQuestions.question: A social enterprise differs from a traditional nonprofit primarily because:
options: It pays no taxes
It generates revenue through market activity to fund its social mission
It is owned by the government
It focuses only on environmental causes
quizQuestions.answer: It generates revenue through market activity to fund its social mission
quizQuestions.explanation: Social enterprises use business models to earn income that sustains their social or environmental mission, unlike nonprofits that rely on donations.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following is an example of greenwashing?
options: A company achieving carbon neutrality verified by a third-party auditor
A company labeling a product 'eco-friendly' without any substantiated environmental improvement
A company publishing its full supply chain emissions data
A company switching its fleet to electric vehicles
quizQuestions.answer: A company labeling a product 'eco-friendly' without any substantiated environmental improvement
quizQuestions.explanation: Greenwashing involves making unsubstantiated or misleading environmental claims to appear more sustainable than the company actually is.

quizQuestions.question: A manager who owns stock in a supplier company and approves that supplier's contract without disclosing the ownership is exhibiting:
options: Ethical leadership
A conflict of interest
Whistleblowing
Due diligence
quizQuestions.answer: A conflict of interest
quizQuestions.explanation: A conflict of interest occurs when a personal interest could inappropriately influence a professional decision.

quizQuestions.question: Ethical leadership in organizations is best demonstrated by:
options: Setting high profit targets for employees to reach
Modeling ethical behavior and creating accountability systems that reward integrity
Publicly criticizing employees who raise ethical concerns
Delegating all ethical decisions to the legal team
quizQuestions.answer: Modeling ethical behavior and creating accountability systems that reward integrity
quizQuestions.explanation: Ethical leaders set a personal example and build organizational structures that incentivize and protect ethical conduct.

quizQuestions.question: Bribery in business contexts refers to:
options: Paying employees above-market wages to retain talent
Offering money or gifts to improperly influence a decision or gain an unfair advantage
Providing discounts to long-standing customers
Sponsoring community events to build brand awareness
quizQuestions.answer: Offering money or gifts to improperly influence a decision or gain an unfair advantage
quizQuestions.explanation: Bribery involves using financial inducements to corrupt decision-making processes, violating both legal standards and ethical norms.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following is a key reason employees often hesitate to blow the whistle on misconduct?
options: They disagree with the company's mission statement
They fear retaliation such as termination, demotion, or professional blacklisting
They prefer to report misconduct to the media first
They are required to wait for an annual ethics review
quizQuestions.answer: They fear retaliation such as termination, demotion, or professional blacklisting
quizQuestions.explanation: Despite legal protections in many countries, fear of professional and personal consequences remains the primary barrier to whistleblowing.

quizQuestions.question: An organization with a strong ethical culture is likely to exhibit which characteristic?
options: Employees rarely discuss ethics because everything is handled through legal compliance
Leaders reward employees who meet targets regardless of how results were achieved
Employees feel safe raising concerns without fear of retaliation
Ethics training is conducted once at hiring and not revisited
quizQuestions.answer: Employees feel safe raising concerns without fear of retaliation
quizQuestions.explanation: Psychological safety to raise ethical concerns is a hallmark of organizations with genuine ethical cultures rather than surface-level compliance.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following practices best describes greenwashing?
options: Investing in wind energy to offset factory emissions
Marketing a product as 'natural' while its manufacturing process produces toxic waste
Publishing full life-cycle environmental assessments of products
Partnering with verified environmental organizations to fund conservation
quizQuestions.answer: Marketing a product as 'natural' while its manufacturing process produces toxic waste
quizQuestions.explanation: Greenwashing misleads consumers about environmental benefits while the actual environmental impact remains significant.

quizQuestions.question: The Paris Agreement primarily requires signatory countries to:
options: Ban all fossil fuel use by 2030
Submit nationally determined contributions to reduce greenhouse gas emissions
Transfer 5% of GDP to developing nations for climate adaptation
Nationalize carbon-intensive industries
quizQuestions.answer: Submit nationally determined contributions to reduce greenhouse gas emissions
quizQuestions.explanation: The Paris Agreement operates on voluntary nationally determined contributions (NDCs) — each country sets and updates its own emissions targets.

quizQuestions.question: Ecological responsibility in business ethics holds that firms are accountable to:
options: Only shareholders and government regulators
Future generations and the natural environment, in addition to current stakeholders
Employees and customers but not the physical environment
Legal requirements only, with no additional moral duties to nature
quizQuestions.answer: Future generations and the natural environment, in addition to current stakeholders
quizQuestions.explanation: Ecological responsibility extends business obligations across time (to future people) and beyond human interests (to ecosystems and other species).

quizQuestions.question: A company that genuinely addresses its environmental impact would most likely:
options: Focus only on recycling programs visible to customers
Set science-based emissions targets aligned with the Paris Agreement and report annually against them
Buy carbon offsets to appear neutral without reducing actual emissions
Mention sustainability in its mission statement without changing operations
quizQuestions.answer: Set science-based emissions targets aligned with the Paris Agreement and report annually against them
quizQuestions.explanation: Genuine sustainability action involves measurable, science-based targets with transparent reporting — not cosmetic gestures.

quizQuestions.question: The dilemma of sustainability in business often centers on the tension between:
options: Short-term profit maximization and long-term environmental and social viability
Increasing wages and maintaining customer satisfaction
Expanding globally and complying with domestic regulations
Innovation speed and patent protection timelines
quizQuestions.answer: Short-term profit maximization and long-term environmental and social viability
quizQuestions.explanation: The core sustainability dilemma is that actions that protect the environment and communities often impose short-term costs that reduce immediate profitability.

quizQuestions.question: Under GDPR, companies must obtain user data by:
options: Including data collection terms in a long terms-of-service document
Obtaining freely given, specific, informed, and unambiguous consent
Assuming consent unless the user actively opts out
Collecting all data as long as it is anonymized afterward
quizQuestions.answer: Obtaining freely given, specific, informed, and unambiguous consent
quizQuestions.explanation: GDPR requires affirmative, informed, specific consent — pre-ticked boxes and bundled consent do not meet the standard.

quizQuestions.question: Algorithmic bias is most accurately defined as:
options: Intentional errors inserted into algorithms by malicious programmers
Systematic and unfair discrimination produced by an algorithm, often stemming from biased training data
The tendency of algorithms to favor accuracy over speed
Differences in results caused by varying hardware capabilities
quizQuestions.answer: Systematic and unfair discrimination produced by an algorithm, often stemming from biased training data
quizQuestions.explanation: Algorithmic bias produces outputs that unfairly disadvantage certain groups, typically because the training data reflects historical human biases.

quizQuestions.question: Surveillance capitalism, as described by Shoshana Zuboff, refers to:
options: Government use of CCTV cameras in public spaces
A business model that commodifies personal data to predict and modify human behavior for profit
Employee monitoring software used by corporations
Academic research on online privacy behaviors
quizQuestions.answer: A business model that commodifies personal data to predict and modify human behavior for profit
quizQuestions.explanation: Zuboff describes surveillance capitalism as the unilateral claiming of human experience as raw material for data products sold to advertisers and others.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following best represents an ethical AI deployment practice?
options: Deploying a model quickly before competitors regardless of bias testing
Testing for bias across demographic groups, publishing results, and establishing an appeal process for affected users
Keeping the model's decision logic proprietary to prevent manipulation
Using AI to automate all hiring decisions to remove human judgment
quizQuestions.answer: Testing for bias across demographic groups, publishing results, and establishing an appeal process for affected users
quizQuestions.explanation: Ethical AI deployment involves transparency, fairness testing, and accountability mechanisms to address harms.

quizQuestions.question: The right to be forgotten (right to erasure) under GDPR allows individuals to:
options: Demand that a company store their data indefinitely
Request deletion of their personal data under certain conditions
Access any data a government holds about them
Prevent all online mentions of their name
quizQuestions.answer: Request deletion of their personal data under certain conditions
quizQuestions.explanation: Article 17 of GDPR grants the right to erasure — individuals can request data deletion when it is no longer necessary, consent is withdrawn, or other specific conditions are met.

quizQuestions.question: Cultural relativism in ethics holds that:
options: All cultures share the same fundamental moral values
Ethical standards are culturally determined and cannot be judged from outside a culture
Western ethical frameworks provide universal moral standards
International law supersedes local cultural norms in all cases
quizQuestions.answer: Ethical standards are culturally determined and cannot be judged from outside a culture
quizQuestions.explanation: Cultural relativism claims that what is morally right is defined within cultural contexts, making cross-cultural moral criticism problematic.

quizQuestions.question: A key criticism of cultural relativism in a business ethics context is that it:
options: Overemphasizes legal obligations over cultural norms
Can justify tolerating serious human rights abuses such as forced labor as 'culturally normal'
Provides too rigid a framework for multinational operations
Ignores profit motives in evaluating corporate behavior
quizQuestions.answer: Can justify tolerating serious human rights abuses such as forced labor as 'culturally normal'
quizQuestions.explanation: If all ethical standards are culturally relative, corporations could use cultural difference to rationalize exploitation, undermining universal human rights protections.

quizQuestions.question: Supply chain ethics most directly concerns:
options: The efficiency of logistics and inventory systems
The labor and environmental conditions of all parties involved in producing a product from raw material to consumer
Patent protections for products designed in high-income countries
The marketing strategies used to sell products in new markets
quizQuestions.answer: The labor and environmental conditions of all parties involved in producing a product from raw material to consumer
quizQuestions.explanation: Supply chain ethics extends corporate responsibility to all tiers of production — not just the company's own operations but also its suppliers and their suppliers.

quizQuestions.question: Which argument best supports the existence of universal ethical standards across cultures?
options: Multinational corporations need consistent rules to maximize profits globally
Fundamental human rights — like freedom from torture and slavery — appear across nearly all legal and philosophical traditions
Cultural norms are increasingly converging due to globalization
Western countries provide most global development aid, so their standards should apply
quizQuestions.answer: Fundamental human rights — like freedom from torture and slavery — appear across nearly all legal and philosophical traditions
quizQuestions.explanation: Cross-cultural convergence on core human rights provides an empirical basis for moral universalism beyond any single cultural tradition.

quizQuestions.question: An ILO (International Labour Organization) core labor standard includes:
options: A mandatory global minimum wage of $15 per hour
Freedom of association and the right to collective bargaining
A maximum workweek of 35 hours for all countries
Prohibition of all performance-based pay structures
quizQuestions.answer: Freedom of association and the right to collective bargaining
quizQuestions.explanation: ILO core standards include freedom of association, collective bargaining rights, elimination of forced labor, child labor, and discrimination — not specific wage or hour mandates.

quizQuestions.question: Which advertising technique is considered deceptive under FTC guidelines?
options: Listing all product ingredients on the label
Making a product claim without adequate evidence to support it
Comparing your product's price to a competitor's using verified data
Displaying customer reviews that include both positive and negative feedback
quizQuestions.answer: Making a product claim without adequate evidence to support it
quizQuestions.explanation: The FTC requires that advertising claims be substantiated by competent and reliable evidence — unsubstantiated claims are deceptive.

quizQuestions.question: The FTC's disclosure requirements for influencer marketing exist primarily to:
options: Increase government tax revenue from social media advertising
Ensure audiences know when content is paid promotion rather than genuine personal opinion
Limit the number of sponsored posts any influencer can publish
Protect influencers from being underpaid by brand partners
quizQuestions.answer: Ensure audiences know when content is paid promotion rather than genuine personal opinion
quizQuestions.explanation: Material connections between influencers and brands must be disclosed so consumers can evaluate the objectivity of the recommendation.

quizQuestions.question: Targeting children with advertising for unhealthy food is ethically problematic primarily because:
options: Children have more disposable income than adults
Children lack the cognitive development to recognize and critically evaluate persuasive intent
Regulations in all countries prohibit advertising to children
Children prefer unhealthy food regardless of advertising
quizQuestions.answer: Children lack the cognitive development to recognize and critically evaluate persuasive intent
quizQuestions.explanation: Children cannot adequately distinguish advertising from information or evaluate commercial intent, making them a vulnerable population warranting special ethical protection.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following is an example of 'native advertising'?
options: A TV commercial that clearly says 'Paid Advertisement' at the start
A sponsored article written to look like editorial news content, with minimal or hidden disclosure
A billboard with a company logo in a public space
A pop-up ad that blocks screen content until dismissed
quizQuestions.answer: A sponsored article written to look like editorial news content, with minimal or hidden disclosure
quizQuestions.explanation: Native advertising blends paid promotional content into editorial formats, potentially deceiving readers who assume they are reading unbiased journalism.

quizQuestions.question: Which marketing practice would most clearly violate ethical norms even if it were technically legal?
options: Offering a loyalty program with genuine discounts for repeat customers
Marketing high-interest payday loans exclusively in low-income neighborhoods using messaging that downplays total repayment cost
Running a sale during a major holiday season
Advertising a product using celebrity endorsements with disclosed partnerships
quizQuestions.answer: Marketing high-interest payday loans exclusively in low-income neighborhoods using messaging that downplays total repayment cost
quizQuestions.explanation: Deliberately targeting vulnerable populations with misleading messaging exploits an information asymmetry and is ethically objectionable even where legal.

quizQuestions.question: Insider trading involves:
options: A company executive buying stock in a competitor after reading public news
Trading securities based on material, non-public information in breach of a duty of trust
Selling shares during a company-mandated blackout period for technical reasons
An employee investing in index funds that include their employer's stock
quizQuestions.answer: Trading securities based on material, non-public information in breach of a duty of trust
quizQuestions.explanation: Insider trading exploits information unavailable to other investors, violating market fairness and securities law.

quizQuestions.question: The Sarbanes-Oxley Act (SOX) was enacted primarily in response to:
options: The 2008 financial crisis caused by mortgage-backed securities
Accounting scandals at Enron and WorldCom that involved fraudulent financial reporting
Insider trading by hedge fund managers in the 1990s
Environmental violations by energy companies in the Gulf of Mexico
quizQuestions.answer: Accounting scandals at Enron and WorldCom that involved fraudulent financial reporting
quizQuestions.explanation: SOX established stricter standards for public company accounting, auditing, and financial disclosure following the Enron and WorldCom collapses.

quizQuestions.question: Predatory lending is unethical primarily because it:
options: Charges interest rates below the market rate, distorting the market
Exploits information asymmetry to trap financially vulnerable borrowers in unaffordable debt
Requires too much documentation from loan applicants
Limits competition by refusing to lend to new customers
quizQuestions.answer: Exploits information asymmetry to trap financially vulnerable borrowers in unaffordable debt
quizQuestions.explanation: Predatory lenders use complex or deceptive terms to extract maximum value from borrowers who lack the information or options to protect themselves.

quizQuestions.question: Impact investing differs from traditional investing primarily because:
options: It only includes government bonds and treasury notes
It intentionally seeks measurable social or environmental benefits alongside financial returns
It avoids all risk by investing only in stable, mature industries
It is regulated differently from ordinary equity investment
quizQuestions.answer: It intentionally seeks measurable social or environmental benefits alongside financial returns
quizQuestions.explanation: Impact investing explicitly targets positive social or environmental outcomes as a goal alongside profitability, not merely as a screening criterion.

quizQuestions.question: A company's board of directors failing to question auditors' assurances of financial accuracy is an example of:
options: Active fraud
Poor corporate governance and failure of fiduciary oversight
Appropriate delegation of financial responsibility
Legal compliance with auditing standards
quizQuestions.answer: Poor corporate governance and failure of fiduciary oversight
quizQuestions.explanation: Board members have a fiduciary duty to shareholders to exercise independent oversight — uncritical acceptance of auditor assurances is a governance failure.

quizQuestions.question: Decision-making under moral uncertainty is best approached by:
options: Always choosing the option that is legal, regardless of its ethical implications
Considering multiple ethical frameworks and looking for convergence among them
Deferring all difficult decisions to legal counsel
Choosing the option that maximizes short-term financial gain
quizQuestions.answer: Considering multiple ethical frameworks and looking for convergence among them
quizQuestions.explanation: When facing moral uncertainty, identifying options that multiple ethical frameworks endorse provides greater confidence in the choice.

quizQuestions.question: Risk assessment in ethical decision-making involves:
options: Calculating only the financial cost of each option
Identifying who might be harmed, how severely, and how likely harm is to occur for each alternative
Selecting the action with the lowest legal liability
Polling employees to determine the most popular option
quizQuestions.answer: Identifying who might be harmed, how severely, and how likely harm is to occur for each alternative
quizQuestions.explanation: Ethical risk assessment maps potential harms, their probability, and their severity across all affected parties — not just financial exposure.

quizQuestions.question: Moral residue refers to:
options: The portion of a decision that is illegal but not punished
A lingering sense of moral regret or responsibility that remains even after making the best available choice
Outdated ethical rules that no longer apply to modern business
The amount of profit left after all ethical costs are subtracted
quizQuestions.answer: A lingering sense of moral regret or responsibility that remains even after making the best available choice
quizQuestions.explanation: Even a justified decision under moral uncertainty may cause unavoidable harm — moral residue is the ethical remainder that demands acknowledgment.

quizQuestions.question: When two ethical principles you hold conflict — for example, honesty and preventing harm — the most defensible approach is to:
options: Always prioritize honesty because it is a universal rule
Always prioritize preventing harm because consequences matter most
Weigh the specific circumstances and apply the framework most relevant to the type of harm at stake
Consult your company's legal team to determine the correct answer
quizQuestions.answer: Weigh the specific circumstances and apply the framework most relevant to the type of harm at stake
quizQuestions.explanation: Moral conflicts require contextual judgment — no single rule automatically overrides all others; wise reasoning considers the nature and stakes of the specific dilemma.

quizQuestions.question: A company considering deploying an imperfect but life-saving medical AI tool faces a dilemma because:
options: The tool will certainly generate negative press if announced
Deploying it may save lives but also cause harm to some patients, while not deploying it also causes harm through inaction
The tool's patent will expire before it can be marketed
Employees are unlikely to be trained to use it effectively
quizQuestions.answer: Deploying it may save lives but also cause harm to some patients, while not deploying it also causes harm through inaction
quizQuestions.explanation: This is a genuine moral dilemma: both action and inaction carry harm, requiring careful weighing of benefits, risks, and obligations to all affected parties.

quizQuestions.question: The primary mechanism of Enron's fraud was:
options: Inflating revenues by selling overpriced energy contracts to customers
Using special purpose entities to hide debt and inflate reported earnings
Bribing government regulators to obtain energy contracts
Paying dividends from borrowed funds to deceive investors
quizQuestions.answer: Using special purpose entities to hide debt and inflate reported earnings
quizQuestions.explanation: Enron created off-balance-sheet entities to conceal billions in debt and make profits appear far larger than they were.

quizQuestions.question: Volkswagen's 'Dieselgate' scandal is primarily an example of:
options: Financial fraud involving falsified earnings reports
Deliberate engineering deception to pass emissions tests the vehicles could not legitimately meet
Bribery of environmental regulators across multiple countries
False advertising about fuel economy without engineering manipulation
quizQuestions.answer: Deliberate engineering deception to pass emissions tests the vehicles could not legitimately meet
quizQuestions.explanation: VW installed defeat devices that detected test conditions and switched to a low-emissions mode only during testing, hiding actual on-road emissions.

quizQuestions.question: Nike's sweatshop controversy in the 1990s primarily raised which ethical issue?
options: Insider trading by Nike executives in supplier companies
Using contract suppliers with extremely poor labor conditions while distancing the brand from direct responsibility
False advertising about the athletic performance of Nike products
Price-fixing agreements with competitor sportswear brands
quizQuestions.answer: Using contract suppliers with extremely poor labor conditions while distancing the brand from direct responsibility
quizQuestions.explanation: Nike's use of independent contractors allowed it to disclaim direct responsibility for labor abuses while benefiting from low-cost production.

quizQuestions.question: The Facebook/Cambridge Analytica scandal involved:
options: Facebook executives selling stock based on non-public user data metrics
A third-party app harvesting data on 87 million users without consent, which was then used for political targeting
Facebook deliberately spreading false news articles to increase engagement
Cambridge Analytica hacking Facebook's servers to obtain user data
quizQuestions.answer: A third-party app harvesting data on 87 million users without consent, which was then used for political targeting
quizQuestions.explanation: The data was collected through a legitimate-seeming personality app that exploited Facebook's API permissions to harvest friends' data, which was then sold to Cambridge Analytica.

quizQuestions.question: A common root cause across major corporate ethics scandals is:
options: Insufficient marketing investment that created financial pressure
A culture where results are rewarded regardless of how they are achieved, combined with weak oversight
Over-regulation that forced companies to find workarounds
Rapid technological change that outpaced employees' abilities
quizQuestions.answer: A culture where results are rewarded regardless of how they are achieved, combined with weak oversight
quizQuestions.explanation: High-pressure performance cultures combined with inadequate governance create environments where ethical violations go unchecked and even become normalized.

quizQuestions.question: The first step in constructing a strong ethical argument is:
options: Stating your conclusion as forcefully as possible
Clearly defining the ethical dilemma and identifying all parties affected
Citing the highest-status authority who agrees with your position
Selecting the framework that most easily supports your preferred outcome
quizQuestions.answer: Clearly defining the ethical dilemma and identifying all parties affected
quizQuestions.explanation: Rigorous ethical reasoning begins with precise problem definition and stakeholder identification before evaluating options or applying frameworks.

quizQuestions.question: When defending an ethical position, the strongest response to a counterargument is to:
options: Dismiss the counterargument as coming from a biased source
Acknowledge its strongest version, then explain why your position still stands despite it
Ignore it and repeat your original point with more emphasis
Cite a different authority who disagrees with the counterargument
quizQuestions.answer: Acknowledge its strongest version, then explain why your position still stands despite it
quizQuestions.explanation: Engaging with the strongest version of the opposition (steelmanning) demonstrates intellectual honesty and makes your own position more credible.

quizQuestions.question: A well-constructed ethical position paper should include:
options: Only the author's personal moral intuitions without reference to frameworks
A clear claim, supporting reasoning with at least one ethical framework, engagement with counterarguments, and evidence
A list of all possible perspectives without reaching any conclusion
Legal citations only, since ethics must ultimately defer to law
quizQuestions.answer: A clear claim, supporting reasoning with at least one ethical framework, engagement with counterarguments, and evidence
quizQuestions.explanation: Strong ethical writing combines a clear thesis, systematic reasoning, honest engagement with opposing views, and empirical or philosophical evidence.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following ethical dilemmas remains genuinely unresolved in contemporary business ethics?
options: Whether slavery is morally wrong
Whether companies should be required to pay living wages to gig workers classified as independent contractors
Whether fraud is unethical
Whether physical safety conditions in factories should meet legal standards
quizQuestions.answer: Whether companies should be required to pay living wages to gig workers classified as independent contractors
quizQuestions.explanation: Gig worker classification and compensation involves genuine tensions between flexibility, economic efficiency, and workers' rights that remain actively debated legally and ethically.

quizQuestions.question: The ability to identify and articulate the strongest version of an opposing argument before refuting it is called:
options: Ad hominem reasoning
Steelmanning
Circular reasoning
Appeal to authority
quizQuestions.answer: Steelmanning
quizQuestions.explanation: Steelmanning means presenting the most charitable and strongest version of an opposing argument — the opposite of the straw man fallacy.
